blockdesc-pipe-bend*: A 90° bend in a pipe run.
blockdesc-pipe-passthroughbend-*: A bent pipe set in masonry.
blockdesc-pipe-outlet*: Connects a pipe run to a machine's port face.
blockdesc-pipe-fluidintake-*: Draws fresh water into the pipe network.
blockdesc-boilercornish-*: Normal single-flue boiler for low steam production.
blockdesc-boilerlancashire-*: Larger twin-flue boiler for high steam production.
blockdesc-enginecornish-*: High-pressure beam engine that's steam intake can be throttled. Drives one attached sub-machine.
blockdesc-enginewatt-*: Low-pressure beam engine — the first step into steam power. Drives one attached sub-machine.
blockdesc-enginefluidpump-*: Engine-driven pump. Transfers water from intake line to pressurised output line.
blockdesc-enginempgenerator-*: Engine-driven crank that turns axles, supplying mechanical power to machines.
blockdesc-steamcondenser-*: Condenses steam from its steam line back into water on its water line.
chimney-info-venting: Venting pipe network: {0:F0} L/s of gas
chimney-info-idle: Capping pipe network (no gas to vent)
boiler-info-needswater: Needs at least {0:F0} L of water to start boiling
engine-throttle-max: The control rods are already wide open (High).
engine-throttle-min: The control rods are already fully closed (Low).
engine-repair-wrench: Hold a wrench to repair the engine.
structure-incomplete-count: Structure is not complete. {0} blocks are missing or incorrect.
game:placefailure-ppex-fluidintake-nowat: The fluid intake must be placed directly on top of a water block.
blockhelp-boiler-lid: Hold to open or close the lid
handbook-steampower-text:
Pipes and Power Expanded adds pipe networks and modular steam machinery. The path into steam runs: water supply → boiler → engine → sub-machine. This article covers the pipes everything breathes through; see Steam Power: Boilers, Steam Power: Engines and Steam Power: Fittings for the machines.

How pipe networks work
Connected pipes form one network with a shared pool. A network carries one medium at a time — a gas (air, blast, exhaust, steam) or water, never both. Each pipe adds 30 L of capacity; pressure is simply volume over capacity, shown in atm. The whole network also shares one temperature.

Materials matter. An iron pipe bursts above 5 atm, a steel pipe above 10 atm — and the weakest pipe limits the whole run. An over-pressured run holds for about 30 seconds, then a pipe lets go.

Open ends leak. Any unconnected pipe connector bleeds the network (≈8 L/s of gas, 10 L/s of water), so a leaking run can never build pressure. Cap runs against machine ports, valves or outlets. Standing water in pipes and boilers also slowly evaporates (≈50 L per day).

Building runs. Straights, bends, T- and X-junctions snap their orientation to neighbouring pipes when placed; rotate a piece with a wrench if it guessed wrong. Pipes connect to machines through their port faces (boiler outlets, engine inlets, pump and intake flanges) — butt the pipe straight against the port.
handbook-boilers-text:
Boilers burn coal under a water vessel and push steam into a pipe network. Boiling 1 L of water yields 16 L of steam, at 160-220 °C depending on how full the vessel is (low water boils hotter).

Construction. Place the boiler block, then rightmouse it repeatedly with the required materials in your hotbar to raise it through its construction stages. The boiler also needs a masonry firebox built around its base — ctrl + shift + rightmouse shows the outline, and the chat reports what is missing.

Cornish Boiler (the starter) — stages consume:
- 42 x Metal Plate (24 must be iron)
- 26 x Metal Nails & Strips
- 14 x Metal Rod
- 48 x Fire Brick
Firebox masonry: 33 x Fire Brick Blocks, 1 x Coke Oven Door, 2 x Fire Brick Pipe Passthrough, 1 x Fire Brick Passthrough Bend (up), 1 x Fire Brick Pipe Outlet (up), one empty slot for the coal pile.
Output: up to 32 L/s of steam, chokes at 5 atm. Vessel holds 800 L; needs 150-500 L of water.

Lancashire Boiler (the upgrade) — stages consume:
- 102 x Steel Plate
- 66 x Metal Nails & Strips
- 12 x Metal Rod
- 64 x Fire Brick
Firebox masonry: 39 x Fire Brick Blocks plus the same door, passthroughs and outlet as the Cornish.
Output: up to 48 L/s of steam, chokes at 12 atm. Vessel holds 1200 L; needs 200-800 L of water.

Operating a boiler
1. Hold rightmouse on the lid to open it, pour in water (buckets, or pipe pressurised water into the intake from a fluid pump — it auto-fills to a safe level), and close the lid.
2. Lay a coal pile in the firebox slot behind the door and ignite it.
3. After about three minutes of heating the boiler starts boiling and pushes steam out its top outlet — pipe it to your engines.
4. The firebox exhaust must breathe: vent its outlet through a chimney or pipe. If exhaust back-pressure rises above ~0.8 atm the fire goes out.

Dangers. A boiler that keeps firing with nowhere to push steam sits at its choke pressure for ~30 seconds, then explodes. An open lid vents steam harmlessly (≈200 L/s) — your emergency release. An unpiped steam outlet just leaks. Feeding pressurised water into a boiling vessel flashes extra steam and spikes the pressure — throttle pumped feed water with a valve. The Cornish boiler's 5 atm ceiling is above the Watt engine's 4 atm breaking point: put a pressure valve between boiler and engine and you have a self-regulating line.
handbook-engines-text:
Engines take steam from a pipe network and drive one attached sub-machine. Place the engine first (it raises through right-click construction stages, like boilers), then place its sub-machine in the marked cell beside it — it snaps into the right facing on its own.

Watt Engine — the low-pressure starter.
- Runs at 2-4 atm inlet steam; above 4 atm it wears toward a break (~60 s grace).
- Draws 30 L/s of steam, delivers 0.3 power, and dribbles ~1 L/s of hot condensate out its drain — pipe it away or it spills on the floor.
- Construction stages consume: 66 x Metal Plate, 42 x Metal Rod, 14 x Metal Nails & Strips.

Cornish Engine — the high-pressure workhorse.
- Runs at 6-8 atm. Its control rods set the working rate: Low / Normal / High draw 8 / 16 / 32 L/s of steam and deliver 0.5 / 1 / 2 power.
- Construction stages consume: 46 x Metal Plate, 42 x Metal Rod, 14 x Metal Nails & Strips.

Sub-machines (one per engine, placed in the engine's drive cell):
- MP Generator — turns axles. It is a constant-power source: within the engine's rated load it holds normal speed; pile on more machines and the whole network slows; past roughly double the rated load it overstresses and stops. A Watt engine comfortably runs a couple of helve hammers; a Cornish engine on High runs a whole workshop.
- Fluid Pump — transfers water from its intake line into its output line, about 5 L/s at full engine power. Its output is pressurised to ~75% of the engine's inlet steam pressure — enough to push water up into a boiler.
- Cornish Air Blower (from Steelmaking Expanded) — pressurises air into Blast for the blast furnace and Bessemer converter.

Breakage and repair. An engine run above its pressure band for too long breaks: it stops and leaks steam. Look at it while holding a wrench to see the repair bill, then rightmouse with the materials in your hotbar to fix it. Mind the Cornish-boiler-into-Watt-engine trap: that boiler can push 5 atm, the Watt breaks at 4 — regulate with a pressure valve.
handbook-fittings-text:
The smaller blocks that shape a pipe network:

Valve — a hand-operated shut-off in the line. rightmouse toggles it: open, the line flows through; closed, the network is severed at the valve and the two sides become independent. Sits flush against machine ports.

Pressure Valve — a directional overflow. It holds back its input side until the pressure rises above its gate pressure, then lets the excess through to its output side; wrench it to flip the direction, rightmouse / ctrl + rightmouse to raise or lower the gate. With nothing piped on the output it vents the excess like a leak. The essential regulator between a strong boiler and a weak engine.

Steam Condenser — steam does not condense inside pipes, no matter how long the run; it only turns back into water here. The condenser pulls up to 30 L/s of steam from its steam port and adds the condensed water (1 L per 16 L of steam) to its separate water line, which passes through it at up to 50 L/s. Use it to reclaim boiler feed water and to relieve steam pressure.

Fluid Intake — draws fresh water from open water into the network. It must sit directly on a full cube of water at least 3 blocks deep, and refuses to work within 6 blocks of another intake. Feed it into a fluid pump to lift the water onward.

Passthroughs & Outlets — masonry blocks with a pipe cast through them, so structure walls (boiler fireboxes, cowper stoves, furnaces) can be built across a pipe run. An outlet ends a run against a machine port face; topped with a vanilla chimney it instead vents the network's gas to the sky at ~16 L/s — the standard way to exhaust a firebox.
handbook-steampowerguide-text:
A minimal working steam line, in build order:

1. Water: place a Fluid Intake on a pond (full 3-deep water below it).
2. Boiler: build a Cornish Boiler with its firebox; fill it with water (buckets work for the first run) and light a coal pile behind its door. Vent the firebox outlet with a chimney.
3. Engine: build a Watt Engine nearby and pipe the boiler's steam outlet to the engine inlet through a Pressure Valve gated at ~2-3 atm (the Cornish boiler can out-pressure a Watt engine).
4. Sub-machine: drop a Fluid Pump or MP Generator into the engine's drive cell. Pump: pipe the fluid intake to its intake side and its output up into the boiler — the water loop closes itself. Generator: hook up axles and run your helve hammers and quern.
5. Pipe the engine's condensate drain back toward the boiler or a drain — and you have a self-feeding steam plant.

From here, scale up: a Lancashire Boiler feeding a Cornish Engine powers the blast furnace air blowers and the Bessemer converter from Steelmaking Expanded.
